content.text: Automatic Prompt Optimization (APO) with DSPy is a paradigm shift in how we develop LLM-powered systems. Instead of hand-engineering natural-language instructions and praying they generalize, DSPy treats the prompt as a learnable parameter: you define what your program should do (via typed signatures), and an optimizer searches the space of possible instructions and few-shot demonstrations to find the combination that maximizes a metric you supply. The result is a compiled program — serializable, reproducible, and often significantly better than anything a human would write from scratch in the same budget of effort.

DSPy was introduced by Omar Khattab et al. at Stanford in 2023. Its name stands for Declarative Self-improving Python. The framework provides a programming model in which modules (analogous to layers in PyTorch) are composed into pipelines, each module backed by an LLM call described by a Signature. Signatures are field-annotated Python classes that specify input fields, output fields, and an optional docstring instruction. The optimizer — BootstrapFewShot, MIPRO, COPRO, or others — then jointly tunes the instructions and the few-shot examples embedded in each module's prompt, without gradient descent or any model weight updates.

This places DSPy in a category distinct from both traditional prompt engineering (manual, non-systematic) and fine-tuning (expensive, requires large labeled datasets, modifies weights). It occupies the middle ground: data-driven, systematic, prompt-level optimization that works with any frozen LLM via its API.

Manually written prompts are brittle. A system prompt that scores 78 % on a task when the model is GPT-4o may drop to 61 % when the deployment moves to a smaller model or when the model is updated. The prompt was implicitly tuned to one model's idiosyncrasies, and those do not transfer. Worse, prompts are typically evaluated informally — a developer runs a handful of examples, the prompt 'feels right', and it ships. Without a formal evaluation harness, regressions are invisible until they surface in production.

DSPy enforces a different discipline. Because optimization is defined against an explicit metric on a fixed dataset, you always know the score, and you can re-optimize whenever the underlying model changes. The separation between the program structure (defined in code) and the prompt text (generated by the optimizer) means that swapping LLMs is as simple as changing the dspy.configure call and re-running compilation. This reproducibility is valuable at any scale, but becomes essential in production systems where dozens of LLM calls compose into a single user-facing response.
Systematic improvement. Scores are measured on a held-out validation set, so progress is objective rather than impressionistic.
Model portability. Recompiling against a new LLM takes minutes, not days of re-engineering.
Reduced prompt engineering labor. Writing signatures is fast; the optimizer handles the tedious work of crafting and iterating on natural-language instructions.

Joint optimization of instructions and demonstrations. MIPRO tunes both the system prompt wording and which few-shot examples to include, which are often entangled — the best examples depend on the instruction phrasing.
Composable pipelines. Multi-hop workflows (retrieve → reason → verify → summarize) are expressed as nested DSPy modules and optimized end-to-end against a single task metric.
Serializable artifacts. Compiled programs are saved to JSON and deployed without any runtime dependency on the training set or the optimizer.
Works with small labeled datasets. BootstrapFewShot can bootstrap useful demonstrations from as few as 10–20 examples by using successful model traces as synthetic training signal.
Upfront LLM cost. Optimization requires N candidate evaluations across T trials, spending O(N×T×|trainset|) LLM calls before a single production call is made.

Requires a metric and labeled examples. If you cannot quantify what 'good' means in code, DSPy cannot optimize for it. Tasks that are purely subjective or safety-critical need human-in-the-loop evaluation.
Compiled programs are opaque. The optimizer may produce instructions that work but are difficult to audit or reason about — a concern in regulated industries.
Optimization is offline. The compiled prompt is static; it does not adapt to distribution shift at inference time without a re-compile cycle.
Tooling maturity. DSPy's API has changed rapidly between versions (1.x to 2.x). Production teams must pin versions carefully and plan for migration effort.
DSPy frames prompt optimization as a discrete search problem. Let
denote a prompt program — a combination of an instruction string and a set of few-shot demonstrations. Given a training set
is the LLM inference function under prompt program

— the space of all possible natural-language instructions combined with subsets of the training set as demonstrations — is combinatorially large and non-differentiable, so gradient methods do not apply.

A Signature is the typed declaration of what an LLM call must do. It specifies input fields (annotated with dspy.InputField) and output fields (annotated with dspy.OutputField), plus an optional class docstring that seeds the instruction. The optimizer treats this docstring as a starting point, not a fixed constraint — it will rewrite it.

A Module wraps one or more Signatures in a forward() method that chains them. Built-in modules include dspy.Predict (direct input-to-output), dspy.ChainOfThought (adds a reasoning field before the answer), dspy.ReAct (implements the Reason + Act loop for tool use), and dspy.ProgramOfThought (emits executable Python). Custom modules inherit from dspy.Module and override forward(). The optimizer sees the module graph and tunes each underlying LM call independently.

The optimizer — BootstrapFewShot in its simplest form — works by running the uncompiled program on each training example, collecting (input, output) traces where the metric is satisfied, and assembling those successful traces as few-shot demonstrations in the prompt. MIPRO extends this by also proposing alternative instruction strings (using a separate meta-LLM call) and running a Bayesian or random search over the Cartesian product of (instruction candidates) × (demo subsets), selecting the combination with the highest validation score.

The diagram below shows the optimization loop. A candidate prompt (instruction + demonstrations) is sent to the LLM, which produces predicted outputs. Those outputs are scored by the metric/evaluator, and the score is fed to the optimizer. The optimizer generates an improved candidate prompt — either by proposing a new instruction (COPRO/MIPRO) or by selecting better demonstrations (BootstrapFewShot). The loop repeats until the termination condition is met.

The training set box (bottom left) feeds labeled examples into the LLM during the bootstrap phase, providing the gold outputs against which the metric is evaluated and from which successful traces are mined for demonstrations.
Your task has a clear, computable metric (exact match, F1, BLEU, ROUGE, pass@k for code, LLM-as-judge score).
You have at least 20–50 labeled examples (inputs + gold outputs) for training, and 10–20 for validation.
You need to port a prompt to multiple LLM providers or model sizes and cannot afford to re-engineer manually for each.
Your pipeline chains multiple LLM calls (retrieval-augmented generation, multi-step reasoning, agent loops) and optimizing each prompt independently misses interaction effects.
You are building a production system where regression testing against a metric is a first-class requirement.
You want to move fast: DSPy lets you express the pipeline structure and get to a reasonable baseline in hours rather than days.

DSPy is less suited when the task is purely creative, when no automatic metric exists, when you have zero labeled data, or when strict auditability of the prompt text is required (e.g., EU AI Act high-risk applications that mandate human review of every prompt).
The implementation follows six well-defined steps. Steps 1–3 are structural (code), step 4 launches the optimizer, and steps 5–6 package and deploy the result.
and a compatible LLM client (openai, anthropic, etc.).
— declare input/output fields and an initial docstring.
— compose Signatures into a dspy.Module subclass.
— create dspy.Example objects with .with_inputs() declaring which fields are inputs.
— a Python function (example, pred, trace) → float or bool.
— instantiate BootstrapFewShot or MIPROv2 and call .compile().

# Install DSPy and a supported LLM client
pip install dspy-ai openai

# For Anthropic Claude support
pip install dspy-ai anthropic

import dspy

# 1. Configure the language model
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# 2. Define a Signature — the input/output contract
class QuestionAnswer(dspy.Signature):
    """Answer a question with a short factual answer."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField(desc="The question to answer")
    answer: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="A concise, accurate answer")

# 3. Wrap it in a Module (ChainOfThought adds a reasoning field)
qa_module = dspy.ChainOfThought(QuestionAnswer)

# 4. Run it zero-shot (no optimization yet)
result = qa_module(question="What is the capital of France?")
print(result.answer)   # Paris
print(result.reasoning)  # step-by-step chain-of-thought

This example shows the complete MIPROv2 optimization workflow on a sentiment classification task. MIPROv2 is the recommended optimizer when you have at least 30–50 training examples and want instruction optimization in addition to few-shot selection.

import dspy
from dspy.evaluate import Evaluate
from dspy.teleprompt import MIPROv2

# --- 1. Configure LM ---
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# --- 2. Define signature and module ---
class Classify(dspy.Signature):
    """Classify the sentiment of a product review."""
    review: str = dspy.InputField()
    sentiment: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="positive, negative, or neutral")

class SentimentClassifier(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        self.classify = dspy.ChainOfThought(Classify)

    def forward(self, review):
        return self.classify(review=review)

# --- 3. Load labeled training data ---
trainset = [
    dspy.Example(review="Absolutely love it!", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Broke after a week.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="It's okay, nothing special.", sentiment="neutral").with_inputs("review"),
    # ... more examples
]
valset = trainset[:10]  # small validation split

# --- 4. Define a metric ---
def exact_match(example, pred, trace=None):
    return example.sentiment.lower() == pred.sentiment.lower()

# --- 5. Run the optimizer ---
optimizer = MIPROv2(
    metric=exact_match,
    auto="medium",   # controls num_candidates and num_trials
    verbose=True,
)
compiled_classifier = optimizer.compile(
    SentimentClassifier(),
    trainset=trainset,
    valset=valset,
)

# --- 6. Use the compiled (optimized) module ---
result = compiled_classifier(review="The battery life is incredible!")
print(result.sentiment)  # positive

BootstrapFewShot is the fastest optimizer to start with. It runs no meta-LLM calls for instruction generation; it only selects demonstrations. Compilation typically completes in 1–5 minutes for small datasets. The save/load pattern shown below is essential for production: compile once, deploy the JSON artifact.

import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot

lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

class SummarizeDocument(dspy.Signature):
    """Summarize the document into a single paragraph."""
    document: str = dspy.InputField()
    summary: str = dspy.OutputField()

class Summarizer(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        self.summarize = dspy.ChainOfThought(SummarizeDocument)

    def forward(self, document):
        return self.summarize(document=document)

def rouge_metric(example, pred, trace=None):
    """Simple word-overlap proxy for ROUGE-1."""
    ref = set(example.summary.lower().split())
    hyp = set(pred.summary.lower().split())
    if not hyp:
        return 0.0
    precision = len(ref & hyp) / len(hyp)
    recall = len(ref & hyp) / len(ref) if ref else 0.0
    if precision + recall == 0:
        return 0.0
    return 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)

trainset = [
    dspy.Example(
        document="Climate change is causing sea levels to rise...",
        summary="Rising sea levels are a key impact of climate change."
    ).with_inputs("document"),
    # ... more examples
]

teleprompter = BootstrapFewShot(metric=rouge_metric, max_bootstrapped_demos=4)
compiled = teleprompter.compile(Summarizer(), trainset=trainset)

# Save for reuse
compiled.save("summarizer_compiled.json")

# Later: load and run
loaded = Summarizer()
loaded.load("summarizer_compiled.json")
print(loaded(document="New research shows ...").summary)

A common production pattern is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), where an LLM must answer questions using passages retrieved from a vector store. Without optimization, the RAG prompt must instruct the model how to use context, how to handle conflicting passages, and what format to use for the answer — all in a single hand-written instruction. With DSPy, you declare the contract (question + context passages → answer), run MIPROv2 against a QA dataset with citations-grounded answers, and the optimizer discovers that adding a 'cite only what the context says' instruction and two demonstrations of correct context use raises exact-match accuracy by 12–18 percentage points on typical benchmarks.

In a real deployment, the retrieve() function wraps a Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector query. The module forward() method is identical regardless of the retriever implementation, because DSPy modules depend only on the field contracts, not the retrieval mechanism.
Using the validation set during optimization.
If your metric is evaluated on the same examples the optimizer uses to select candidates, you are overfitting to the validation set. Keep a separate held-out test set for final evaluation.
BootstrapFewShot needs enough examples to find traces where the metric passes. With fewer than 10 examples, the bootstrap may find no successful traces and produce no demonstrations.

A metric that returns True for nearly every output gives the optimizer no gradient signal. Make sure your metric discriminates: the compiled program should score meaningfully higher than the uncompiled baseline.
dspy.Example objects require you to declare which fields are inputs with .with_inputs('field1', 'field2'). Omitting this causes silent misuse of fields as both inputs and labels.
Running compiled.predict() on the trainset and reporting that score is a data contamination error. Always evaluate on held-out data.
Optimization is expensive. Always call compiled.save() before deploying. Rerunning compilation is wasteful and may produce a different (possibly worse) result due to sampling variance.
The compiled prompt is optimized for a specific model's response patterns. Switching models without recompiling often degrades performance.
before reaching for MIPROv2. It is faster, cheaper, and often sufficient for tasks where instruction wording is not the bottleneck.
alongside the compiled program. A change in the training set invalidates the compiled artifact.
on a held-out test set before and after compilation to measure the real lift. Report both scores.
via the auto parameter ('light', 'medium', 'heavy') or explicit num_candidates and num_trials to control cost.
during development to see exactly what prompt text the optimizer is trying.
Pin dspy-ai to a specific version
in production. The optimizer behavior changed between 1.x and 2.x and may change again.
into smaller signatures rather than writing one giant instruction. The optimizer handles each sub-task independently and combines the results.
The cost of compilation scales as
is the number of instruction candidates,
is the number of optimization trials, and
is the training set size. For MIPROv2 with auto='medium' defaults (roughly
), a training set of 50 examples, and a pipeline with 2 LLM calls, compilation costs approximately 3,000 LLM calls.

DSPy supports parallelism via the num_threads parameter in dspy.Evaluate and in MIPROv2.compile(). Setting num_threads=8 can reduce wall-clock compilation time by 6–7× on networks with ample rate limits. For very large training sets (>500 examples), subsample randomly for the optimization phase and evaluate on the full set only at the end.

At inference time, compiled programs are zero-overhead compared to hand-written prompts: the compiled JSON is loaded once at startup and the module's forward() executes exactly as many LLM calls as the graph defines — no overhead from the optimizer at runtime.
(PyPI) — the core library. Install with pip install dspy-ai.
— dspy.LM accepts any LiteLLM model string, giving access to 100+ providers with a single API.
— DSPy provides an mlflow autolog integration; compiled programs and their scores are logged as MLflow runs.
— all interoperate with DSPy as retrieval backends; dspy.Retrieve is the standard abstraction.
— a debugging tool that prints the last N LLM calls, including the full prompt and response, to stderr.
The table below compares DSPy against the most common alternatives for systematic prompt optimization.
Human writes and iterates on text
Mines successful traces for few-shot demos
LLM generates candidate instructions; beam search

Compiled programs encode prompt text that the optimizer found effective — and the optimizer has no security awareness. If the training data contains adversarial strings, a compiled program may generalize to them. Always validate the compiled prompt text before deploying to production by inspecting the saved JSON and running it through your organization's prompt security review.

DSPy's ChainOfThought module includes a reasoning field in the model output. This intermediate reasoning is part of the completion and is by default returned to the caller. If you are filtering model outputs for PII or policy violations, apply the filter to the full completion including the reasoning field, not only the final answer field.
Do not pass raw user input as Signature fields without sanitization. Prompt injection via user-controlled inputs is a real risk in DSPy pipelines, exactly as in hand-written prompts.
Treat compiled .json files as code artifacts: store them in version control, apply access controls, and review diffs before deploying updates.
Set a token budget on MIPROv2 compilation in environments where cost overruns have real consequences — use the max_labeled_demos and num_trials parameters to cap spend.

The clearest mental model for DSPy is the compiler analogy. In traditional software, you write source code (high-level intent) and a compiler translates it into machine code (the artifact that runs). In DSPy, you write Signatures and Modules (high-level intent) and the optimizer translates them into compiled prompt programs (the artifact that runs in production). Just as you do not hand-write assembly, you do not hand-write the final prompt text. Just as compiled binaries are tied to a target architecture, compiled DSPy programs are tied to a target model — and you recompile when the model changes.

A second useful frame is hyperparameter search. BootstrapFewShot is analogous to selecting training examples for in-context learning; MIPROv2 is analogous to joint hyperparameter search over (architecture, data augmentation). The metric is the loss function. The training set is the training data. The validation set prevents overfitting. All the intuitions from classical ML model selection apply.
DSPy treats prompts as compiled artifacts, not fixed text — define the contract in code and let the optimizer find the best instructions and demonstrations.
on a validation set, making improvement measurable and regressions detectable.
BootstrapFewShot is the fastest starting point; MIPROv2 achieves higher scores by also searching over instruction phrasing, at greater LLM cost.
Compiled programs are serialized to JSON and load at zero runtime overhead — the optimizer runs only once, offline.
Recompiling when you change the underlying LLM is the correct workflow; compiled prompts are model-specific and do not automatically transfer.
The framework enforces engineering discipline: explicit metrics, versioned data, and separation between program structure and prompt text.
DSPy does not replace fine-tuning for tasks that require deep behavioral change; it is most powerful when you need systematic, reproducible prompt optimization across a family of frozen LLMs.
